attack_summary: An active ICMP flood (direct network flood) originated from a compromised Ubuntu host (UbuntuComputer) using hping3 to send high-rate ICMP echo requests (targeting 7.0.14.254). The agent has elevated (root) privileges and two IP addresses (192.168.1.3 and 203.0.113.2). The attacker remained present throughout the session.

mitigations.name: Insert inline Snort IPS between FortiGate and LAN
mitigations.brief_description: Deleted the direct FortiGate<->switch link, added a pre-configured Snort device, and reconnected FortiGate -> Snort -> Cisco switch so Snort inspects LAN-bound traffic inline. This leverages the pre-configured inline IPS to detect and block ICMP flood traffic.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

mitigations.name: FortiGate deny-ICMP firewall policy for 192.168.1.3
mitigations.brief_description: Created an address object for 192.168.1.3 and added a firewall policy to deny the PING service from port2->port1 for that host (logging enabled). The rule did not stop the ongoing ICMP flood (attack success remained).

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

mitigations.name: Host-based outbound ICMP rate-limit and disable hping3
mitigations.brief_description: On the compromised Ubuntu host inserted iptables OUTPUT rules: a rate-limited ACCEPT for ICMP echo-request (limit 5/sec, burst 10) then DROP for remaining echo-requests, and chmod 000 on /usr/sbin/hping3 to prevent its execution. Rules and binary disable verified on the host.

key_observations: The attacker had elevated (root) access on the Ubuntu host and multiple IP addresses (192.168.1.3 and 203.0.113.2); this complicated single-address firewall blocking.

An inline LAN-side IPS (Snort) is immediately effective for blocking malicious ICMP floods when placed between firewall and internal switch; it was deployed successfully and preserved legitimate connectivity.

The FortiGate deny-ICMP policy targeted only 192.168.1.3 and WAN-bound ICMP, which did not stop the active attack—likely due to alternate source IP/NAT, policy ordering, or the flood using a different egress path.

Host-level controls (iptables + disabling attack binary) immediately prevented local-originated ICMP floods regardless of source IP, but are reversible since the attacker has root access—so they are temporary containment controls.
Defense-in-depth (network IPS + firewall rules + host controls) was used; each layer has different strengths and limitations in a live-response scenario.
overall_conclusion: A layered approach was used to stop the active ICMP flood while leaving the compromised host online. Insertion of an inline Snort IPS (network-level) and host-based egress controls (iptables + disabling hping3) provided immediate effective mitigation. The FortiGate host-specific ICMP deny policy failed to stop the flood due to scope/egress/source-IP mismatch. Although the attack was stopped operationally, the attacker retains root access on the host, so permanent remediation (forensics, credential rotation, reimage/remediation) is still required once live-response constraints are lifted. Recommended next steps: review/confirm FortiGate policy ordering and egress/NAT behavior, monitor Snort logs and firewall logs, make host rules persistent and collect forensic artifacts, and plan full remediation because host-level changes can be reverted by the attacker.
